data.name: Lawrence Ati-Zigi
data.description: Ati-Zigi never really wanted to be a keeper. “My friends made me, and I have lived with it,” he said. “I started playing football at the age of six as an outfield player. But one day we didn’t have a keeper, so I went between the posts, and that was it.” It goes without saying that the switch paid off. He has had a very solid national-team career, representing his country at three Africa Cup of Nations (2019, 2021, 2023) and being first-choice at the 2022 World Cup in Qatar. He stands as the most experienced keeper in the squad this summer, having made his debut in 2018, seven years before the current first-choice, Benjamin Asare. He has always been a fan of drawing and painting, which helps him stay cool on the pitch. "Errors in football don't upset me," he said. "The most important thing is to keep calm. And my hobby fits very well with that. I love drawing everything that comes into my mind, including lots of fun things."

data.name: Alidu Seidu
data.description: Educated at the Jean-Marc Guillou academy in Ivory Coast, Seidu joined Clermont Foot in 2019 and made his first-team debut a year later. In 2024 he moved to Rennes on a four-year deal, but suffered an anterior cruciate ligament injury that November. It has been a long road back. “It was tough because I was in a lot of pain,” he told the newspaper Ouest France. “But I quickly saw it as a challenge. I have been through worse … I grew up in a very tough, very violent area. Being around friends who were into that lifestyle and hanging out on the streets made me a bit aggressive, mean. We walked the streets with knives or machetes, I committed thefts, vandalism, and small-time trafficking. My mother was always crying, and sometimes I ran away at night to avoid my father punishing me.” When he was attacked with a machete during a fight, he had to spend three months in hospital. “I lost a lot of blood, I couldn’t speak … Everyone thought I was going to die. So I’m lucky to have become a player, to be here. I always try to turn the negative side into something positive, it’s in my character. My uncle pushed me towards football because he knew I had something. He always supported me, and football saved my life.”

data.name: Caleb Yirenkyi
data.description: Another product of the famous Right to Dream academy, Yirenkyi joined the Danish side Nordsjælland in 2024 and was the toast of Ghana fans after two appearances in last year’s Kirin Cup. A powerful, skilful and versatile performer, adept box-to-box or as a right-wingback, he is often compared to Michael Essien, who is now one of his coaches in Denmark. On Essien’s influence, Yirenkyi says: “He can coach and impact everyone and anyone. We learn a lot from him and are very grateful to have such a guy.” Yirenkyi arrived at the World Cup after months of transfer speculation linking him with England’s biggest sides. His club and international teammate Prince Amoako Junior told Joy Sports in January: “He doesn’t see himself the way we see him because he’s a very humble guy. But Caleb is insane, he’s crazily good. He’s a superstar, and I tell him so every time.”

data.name: Jonas Adjetey
data.description: After making 29 starts in 32 appearances in his breakthrough campaign and enjoying four years with Basel, Adjetey moved to Wolfsburg for a reported €10m at the start of this year, and made swift progress, improving his consistency, timing his tackles better, and perfecting long diagonal balls. His development, and Mohammed Salisu’s unfortunate anterior cruciate ligament injury, have made him almost an automatic pick for Ghana this summer. He had a tough upbringing in Teshie, a suburb of Accra. “We didn’t really have much,” he told Wolfsburg’s website. “You go to school, play football and help your parents. Three things, every day.” When he needed football boots he had to get up at 5am and go to a construction site with his friends. “I had to work. I helped out there so I could afford them.” Then he lost his father in a car accident. “I am very sad that he can no longer witness what he invested so much time and passion in … My parents gave everything for my sister and me.”

data.name: Thomas Partey
data.description: Part of the Ghana side for a decade, Partey is at his second World Cup. A powerful defensive midfielder, he won the Europa League and appeared in the 2016 Champions League final with Atlético Madrid, and became the most expensive Ghanaian player ever when he joined Arsenal four years later for £45m. Four days after he left Arsenal for Villarreal in 2025 he was charged with five counts of rape and one count of sexual assault relating to alleged incidents between 2021 and 2022. Partey pleaded not guilty to all those charges at Southwark crown court in September, where a trial date was set for 2 November 2026. In February of this year he faced two new counts of rape after another woman came forward to police. In April he pleaded not guilty to those two allegations of rape. In May, his trial was put back to June 2027. His defence lawyer, Jenny Wiltshire, said: “Thomas Partey continues to deny all charges against him. He has cooperated with the police throughout.” On 12 June Fifa said Partey had been denied entry to Canada and would miss Ghana’s opening game against Panama.

data.name: Abdul Mumin
data.description: It was touch and go whether Mumin would make it to North America. He returned to competitive football only in April after 13 months out following an anterior cruciate ligament injury. He sustained the injury just as he was in a golden spell of form, proving himself one of Rayo’s most consistent performers, and it left him facing months of gruelling rehabilitation. But he got there – and his international coach, Carlos Queiroz, was determined to include him for his La Liga knowhow, composure and aerial dominance. He’s a solid role model, too. In 2024, when Rayo beat the minnows Atlético de Lugones 6-0 in the Copa del Rey first round, Mumin spent half-time playing football with the host club’s ballboys and posing for photos with them. The club posted online later: “We will always be happy for Abdul's success. Our ballboys haven't forgotten his kind gesture of playing with them during half-time of the Copa del Rey match.”

data.name: Abdul Fatawu
data.description: Capable of some extraordinary moments, including three goals from the halfway line, one for Ghana’s under-20s, one for the under-23s, and one for Leicester against Ipswich in December last year which, measured at at least 65 yards, was the longest-range Championship goal on record. His manager at the time, Marti Cifuentes, said: “It’s a brilliant moment from Abdul. He tries it a lot in training. He likes to look for those situations. He always pushes himself to be better.” Fatawu told the BBC: “I always try this from long range, and when I hit this one I didn’t have any doubts. I feel amazing.” He sustained an anterior cruciate ligament injury in November 2024 but worked his way back, proved his form, and has again been linked with Premier League clubs.

data.name: Kwasi Sibo
data.description: Until fairly recently, Kwasi was the lesser-known of the Sibo brothers. His brother, Simon, always drew the focus, having captained the local side Liberty Professionals and represented Ghana’s youth national teams in the late 2010s. However, Kwasi truly upstaged him after helping Real Oviedo to secure a return to La Liga after 24 years. Not that Simon has conceded supremacy, though. “When I come down for holidays, Simon still trains me,” says Kwasi. “He is still my coach, and every now and then, he will teach me a trick or two! But me playing for Ghana is the fulfilment of a family dream. My brothers have always wanted one of us to play for the Black Stars.” The name Sibo in the Lambossie language (of the Sissala ethnic group in Ghana’s Upper West Region) means a big family of warriors, and Kwasi is true to that: combative, athletic and relentless on the ball.

data.name: Jordan Ayew
data.description: After 16 years in the national team, Ayew arrives this summer on the back of a gruelling club season with Leicester, whom he joined from Palace in 2024. But while his displays have been mixed in England as the Foxes struggled, his consistently productive form for Ghana has shown what a key role he can still play at international level, with seven goals and six assists during World Cup qualifying. He was also given the captaincy, saying later: “I am living a dream and I don’t know when it will end. I never thought I would become captain, it was never in my mind, but I believe in destiny and I’m hoping for more.” He is the third family member to have captained the Black Stars after his famous father, Abedi Pele, who wore the armband from 1992 to 1998, and his older brother, André, captain from 2019 to 2024.

data.name: Brandon Thomas-Asante
data.description: It has been a prolific 12 months for Thomas-Asante. Last May, he opened his international account against Nigeria in the 2025 Unity Cup, and a year later, he helped spearhead Coventry’s Championship title charge with 13 goals in 33 appearances. It has been a remarkable rise for a player who rose through the ranks from the English Football League and will play top-flight football after 10 seasons as a senior professional. “I’m really eager to keep my head down, keep ready because I’m so passionate about my nation,” Thomas-Asante said on heading to his first major tournament after success with Coventry. He has been rewarded for his great efforts this season with the famous Ghana No 10 shirt, previously donned by Stephen Appiah, who captained the Black Stars to their World Cup record quarter-final finish in 2010, and André Ayew.

data.name: Antoine Semenyo
data.description: Arsenal, Millwall, Reading and Tottenham turned Semenyo down as a youngster, and Palace let him go at 15 after eight weeks on trial. “I’ve never been so upset in my life about football. I was crying to my dad,” he told the Guardian in 2024. “I stopped playing for a year, just focused on school.” But he earned a trial at Bristol City in 2017 via a youth academy programme and signed his first professional contract there. He had three loans spells before establishing himself, then joined Bournemouth for £10m in 2023, and Man City for £64m in January. “Sometimes I get memories flash up on my phone from when I was out on loan, and I compare them with now,” he said. “You go on a trip down memory lane and it is like: ‘Wow, you’ve come a long way.’” He has the word "chosen" tattooed on his back. He told GQ: “I looked at [basketballer] Jayson Tatum's tattoos – he has the name of the street he grew up on and other stuff, and it said ‘chosen’ on his back. I was like: 'You know what? This resembles me. I feel like the chosen one, I feel like God's chosen me to be in this position.'”

data.name: Joseph Anang
data.description: A former England Under-20s keeper, Anang emerged as an unlikely option for Ghana in May last year when he was called up for the Unity Cup at Brentford in London. His debut came last November in a 2-0 loss to Japan in the Kirin Cup. A solid shot-stopper with quick reflexes, he was born in Accra but moved to England with his family as a teenager. “My parents both went abroad at different times to get a better life for our family,” he said in 2024. “My dad was an electrician and went to the US, but he has now retired, then my mum moved to the UK to work first as a cleaner, then she did a nursing course and worked in a care home for people with dementia. I lived with my grandparents and my one sister and two brothers, and followed my mum here a few years later.” He signed a scholarship deal with West Ham in 2017 but did not make a first-team breakthrough in seven years there. After solid loan spells at Stevenage and St Patrick’s, he signed a permanent deal with the League of Ireland Premier Division club in 2024.

data.name: Christopher Bonsu Baah
data.description: Blessed with pace, close control and remarkable dribbling ability, Bonsu-Baah has long been highly rated, with some of the world’s biggest clubs circling him when he was just 14. "I was allowed to trial everywhere: at Borussia Dortmund, Barcelona, Manchester United … But because I was not yet 18, I couldn’t get a professional contract. It’s good that I have a lot of patience," he told De Zondag. In 2023, he left the lower-tier side Shooting Stars in Ghana for Sarpsborg in Norway, but stayed just four months before the Belgian Pro League side Genk signed him on a five-year deal. Last year he swapped Belgium for Saudi Arabia’s Al-Qadsiah, managed by Brendan Rodgers, and made his Black Stars debut.

data.name: Gideon Mensah
data.description: One of the most experienced players in the Black Stars squad, having made his debut in 2019 against South Africa in a 2021 Africa Cup of Nations qualifier in Cape Coast. He does, though, have an error in him, which is why Ghana’s coach, Otto Addo, has been through five other options in the left-back slot, trying Patrick Kpozo, Ebenezer Annan, Derrick Köhn, Abdul Fatawu Hamidu and Isaac Afful. But Mensah’s experience and positivity count for a lot. In 2019 he nearly made a dream loan move to Barcelona, to be deputy to Jordi Alba. When his agent told him about the offer, he recalled in 2024: “I couldn’t resist thinking about the next flight to Spain.” But the deal collapsed when news of the agreement leaked, breaching a confidentiality agreement. “The next day the news was everywhere in Ghana that Barcelona wanted me,” he told Flashscore. “But nobody knows what could happen in the future. The opportunity could come back again.” His Instagram bio: “Keep Praying, Keep Planning, Keep Pushing.”

data.name: Elisha Owusu
data.description: Born in France to Ghanaian parents, Owusu started out in Lyon’s academy. He signed his first professional deal with them in 2017, but didn’t make a breakthrough there, moving instead to Sochaux on loan in 2018, then to Belgium with Gent a year later. He joined current club Auxerre in 2023. A solid, dependable leader in midfield, he has captained Auxerre and brings plenty of know-how to this squad. He made his international debut in the 2022 World Cup playoff tie against Nigeria, and spoke in February about his ambition. “It would be a big blessing to play in the World Cup, but I try not to think about it too much. For me life is about process. I take it day by day, and leave the rest to God.”

data.name: Benjamin Asare
data.description: While at Hearts of Oak’s rivals, Great Olympics, in June 2021, Asare broke his leg in the derby against his current employers. He missed 18 months of football and was advised to consider retiring, but made a remarkable comeback, and within two years was Ghana’s first-choice goalkeeper. His introduction to the national team was a remarkable breakthrough. After Ghana conceded seven goals in six games and finished bottom of their qualifying group for the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations, Asare was drafted in, one of a raft of changes. It made him, at 32, the oldest Hearts of Oak player to make his Ghana debut, and the first of their keepers to represent the national side for 20 years. His impact was stunning: four clean sheets in five matches as Ghana secured World Cup qualification. In November last year he saved Hwang Hee-chan’s penalty in the Kirin Cup against South Korea, cementing his growing reputation.

data.name: Abdul Rahman Baba
data.description: The former Chelsea defender is back for Ghana after a long absence, having been in self-imposed exile since 2023 when he was booed by fans during an Africa Cup of Nations qualifier against Central African Republic in Kumasi. He told Joy Sports last year: “The booing didn’t affect me during the match – as a professional, you learn to deal with that. But after the final whistle, as I walked to the team bus, it really sank in. It was tough to take. So I felt it was the right time to focus on my body and recover properly.” His fine form in Greece last season means he returned to the national setup this summer in a confident mood. He spent eight years on Chelsea’s books from 2015 to 2023 but made only 23 appearances, spending most of that time on loan or in recovery having suffered two serious knee injuries. He nearly gave up after the second, in 2019. "I said to my family I was quitting," he told the BBC in 2024. "But Dr Paco [Biosca, ex-Chelsea medical director] said that he had seen other players come back and promised that if I was patient and mentally strong that I could do it too. I slept on it and then decided I wanted to fight for my career. I am grateful that football has given me a second chance. I'm just trying to enjoy it to the last moment."

data.name: Jerome Opoku
data.description: The Lambeth-born Opoku has had a nomadic route to the top. A Fulham academy product, he suffered an anterior cruciate ligament injury aged 17, then had three loan spells, at Accrington Stanley, Plymouth and the Danish side Vejle, before joining the Portuguese side Arouca in 2022. He moved from there to Turkey’s Istanbul Basaksehir in 2023. His form since has been impressive, and he was linked with a return to the Premier League during the last winter window. He played it down. “As I always say, it’s God’s plan, so I think that if I just keep putting in the performances, whatever comes will kind of reflect on where I could be, or where I should be,” he told World Soccer Talk. “For now, I’m here in Istanbul, and that’s what I’m focused on, but whatever happens is really God’s plan.” He made his Ghana debut in 2023, and said securing World Cup qualification last year was “just amazing … when I was on that pitch, and once the whistle went, I looked around me, I saw everyone's faces, I saw my family's faces … It made me feel like I was a part of something much bigger than just football. You see how much it means to literally everyone in the country.”

data.name: Iñaki Williams
data.description: In 2021 Williams declined initial overtures from the Ghana Football Association. He had played once for Spain already, in a 2016 friendly, and told the Guardian: “I was born in Bilbao. I won’t ever forget my family roots, but I feel Basque and can’t con anyone. I don’t think it would be right to take the place of someone who really deserves it and who feels Ghana 100%.” But when Ghana came calling again having secured World Cup qualification in 2022, Williams changed his mind. “I still wasn’t clear about it until I went to Ghana with my parents, my family and girlfriend. We went to see the country, the family in Accra and Kumasi. It was emotional, like the whole city was waiting when I got out of the car. It was incredible, incredible. And that’s where everything changed. That’s when I went to talk to my grandad. I told him there was a chance that I could play for Ghana at the World Cup and what did he think. He said straight away that he didn’t have long left to live and he dreamed of his grandson playing for Ghana. There was nothing left to think about then.” His younger brother, Nico, also plays for Athletic Bilbao, and for Spain.

data.name: Augustine Boakye
data.description: Boakye left Ghanaian football in 2021, signing a four-year deal at the Austrian club Wolfsberger. After moving to Saint-Étienne three years later, he spoke openly about one of his biggest ambitions. “It would be a dream to play for my country, but I’m not going to demand it and say I have to. The call-up could come today or tomorrow, I don’t know when – but when it comes, it will be good, and I will be ready.” He was made to wait, but it was worth it: his first call-up coming as part of this summer’s squad. He knows his role is to feed the forwards. “I am a No 10 who loves to assist. I love it most when I assist my striker because I think that when the striker scores more it gives the team more confidence. I see the striker as the main man and so when he’s not scoring and he gets frustrated it affects the whole team.” Boakye’s childhood hero was the former Ghana midfielder Sulley Muntari. “He was my idol growing up because my mum loved him so much. So I watched him, and also loved him.”

data.name: Kojo Peprah Oppong
data.description: It has been a meteoric rise. Only three years ago he was still in the second tier of Ghanaian football, with Attram De Visser Academy, a club co-owned by the former Black Star Godwin Attram. That summer he left for the Swedish side IFK Norrköping, where, via a loan spell at GIF Sundsvall, he quickly proved his potential – so much so that in August last year Nice paid €2.5m to sign him. Florian Maurice, sporting director at Nice, said: “What drew us to him was not only his speed, his mobility and his aggression in duels, but also his ability to play in different systems.” He settled fast, playing league and European football, and was soon being linked with Premier League clubs. “I’m surprised,” Oppong told Get French Football News last year. “I didn’t expect to play this much in the beginning, but I’m now playing a lot – and I expect a lot from myself.” He made his Ghana debut in the World Cup qualifier against Comoros in Accra last October. His future looks bright.

data.name: Kamaldeen Sulemana
data.description: Pure pace. During a second-half cameo against Uruguay in the 2022 World Cup, the winger Sulemana clocked 22.18mph, the fastest time recorded by any player at the tournament, surpassing the lightning-quick Alphonso Davies and Nico Williams. “I wasn’t even running fast,” he reflected later. “It was when I was running back, you know, we were down 2-0, and then it was like five minutes to go, and there was a counter against us, so I had to cover back." He joined Atalanta from Southampton for £22m and is in his sixth year with the Black Stars, with this his second World Cup and fourth major tournament overall. He used to be known for his athletic backflip goal celebration, having been taught gymnastics as well as football at Ghana’s Right to Dream academy. But no longer. “My parents told me not to do it any more because it’s dangerous,” he said in October last year. “I did it against Juventus, but it was just an adrenaline rush, spontaneous. I won’t do it again.”

data.name: Derrick Luckassen
data.description: It’s been quite a journey to this World Cup. Born in Amsterdam, Luckassen started out at AZ Alkmaar’s youth academy and made his senior club breakthrough there. He joined their rivals PSV Eindhoven on a five-year deal in 2017, then had a series of loans, at Hertha and Anderlecht, then the Turkish clubs Kasimpasa and Fatih Karagumruk. He moved to Cyprus with Pafos in 2024, helping them win the title in his first season, and, on 5 November 2025, it was his headed goal that gave the club their first ever win in the Champions League proper – a shock league stage 1-0 victory over Villarreal. After representing the Netherlands at youth level without making a senior breakthrough, he finally received his first Ghana call-up this March. He made the final cut for this summer when Alexander Djiku was ruled out with a hamstring injury.

data.name: Ernest Nuamah
data.description: Nuamah emerged as one of Ghana’s most promising offensive talents from Right to Dream, one of West Africa’s most prestigious academies. After developing his creative and pace-driven style in Denmark, he joined Lyon on loan before signing permanently with the Ligue 1 club. In 2024, a deal with Fulham had been agreed but the move collapsed after Nuamah disappeared during his medical. His agent confirmed the player’s deep affection for Lyon, leading the club owner, John Textor, to apologise for attempting to pressure the winger into the transfer. In 2025, he ruptured an anterior cruciate ligament but a year later he needed only three substitute appearances for his club to convince Carlos Queiroz that he should go to the World Cup. The 22-year-old says: “You should expect the Nuamah you know. I’ve trained very hard, and with the support of my teammates, I think I’m ready to give everything I can for this nation. It hasn’t been easy for me, getting an injury. I’ve worked really hard to get to this point and I can’t wait to give my all.”

data.name: Prince Kwabena Adu
data.description: Six years ago Kwabena Adu, then at Ghana’s Bechem United, was named on the Guardian’s Next Generation list of 60 of the best young talents in world football. But in 2021, just before leaving to represent Ghana at the Under-20s Africa Cup of Nations he sustained a career-threatening ligament injury which kept him out for two years. "It was a really difficult moment in my life," Adu told Flashscore last year. "It was tough so I went back to my hometown to stay there and relax my mind. At that point I wasn't thinking about playing football again. Being injured for such a long period made me question a lot of things." But he did recover, working his way back to form and fitness, and made a move to the Czech giants Viktoria Plzen in August 2024. His role model? "My style is very similar to Luis Suárez so I watch his videos a lot. The way Suárez scores, runs, his movement, with and without the ball, and off the pitch. I like everything about him and really learn a lot from him. I even still watch him in the MLS.”

data.name: Marvin Senaya
data.description: At the start of the year not many would have expected the 25-year-old to be in the World Cup squad but, having made his national-team debut in March against Austria, he excelled against Wales in May and is now competing for a spot in the starting XI. The France-born right-back started out at Strasbourg and has worked his way up the leagues – including a spell in Switzerland with Lausanne – before he joined Auxerre in 2025. He could have chosen to play for Togo – his father is Yao Mawuko Senaya, who won 26 caps for the country of his birth between 1998 and 2008. In March, Marvin became one of only a few footballers to have been called up by two national teams for the same international window as both Ghana and Togo included him in their squads. “I feel deeply honoured to represent my country,” he said. “Ghana has a rich footballing history and I will wear the shirt with pride, loyalty and commitment.”
